title: Poulet à la Moutarde
subtitle: French mustard chicken — bone-in pieces braised in white wine, cream, and Dijon
description: Poulet à la moutarde is a staple of French home cooking and bistro menus: bone-in chicken pieces browned in butter, then braised in white wine and stock, finished with a generous measure of Dijon mustard stirred through double cream. The sauce is rich, sharp, and deeply savoury. It takes under an hour from start to finish and needs only rice, noodles, or bread alongside.
sourceNotes: A standard dish of French bourgeois cuisine, codified in multiple French cookery texts of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The cream-and-mustard sauce combination appears consistently in French provincial cooking from Burgundy and the Rhône valley. Referenced in Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961).

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to brown the chicken in a single layer — 28–30 cm diameter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A smooth, sharp French mustard made from brown mustard seeds and white wine. The heat dissipates when the mustard is cooked; add it off the heat (or at the very end of cooking) to preserve its sharp flavour.

ingredients.notes: Or a mix of thighs and drumsticks
Added at the end of cooking

steps: Heat the butter and oil in a wide casserole over a medium-high heat. Season the chicken pieces with salt and pepper. Brown skin-side down for 5 minutes until deep golden, then turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Remove to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Cook the shallots in the remaining fat for 3–4 minutes until soft. Add the garlic for 1 minute.

Pour in the white wine and let it bubble, scraping up the fond. Add the hot chicken stock. Return the chicken pieces skin-side up, partially submerged in the sauce. Cover and simmer gently for 30–35 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken to a plate. Increase the heat to medium-high and reduce the sauce for 5 minutes until slightly thickened. Add the cream and simmer for 3 minutes.
Remove from the heat and stir in the Dijon mustard — do not return to the boil after adding the mustard or the sauce can turn grainy. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Return the chicken to the sauce. Scatter with tarragon and serve.

content.text: goes in off the heat — do not boil the sauce after adding it. High heat breaks the emulsion and can make the sauce grainy. Add, stir, taste, and serve.

Heat {{butter-unsalted}} of butter and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide casserole over a medium-high heat. Season {{chicken-thigh-bone-in-skin-on}} of chicken and brown skin-side down for 5 minutes until deeply golden, then turn for 3 minutes more. Remove to a plate.

Cook the shallots in the remaining fat for 3–4 minutes, then the garlic for 1 minute. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and scrape up the fond. Add {{chicken-stock}} of hot stock. Return the chicken skin-side up, cover, and simmer gently for 30–35 minutes.

Remove the chicken. Increase the heat and reduce the sauce for 5 minutes. Add {{double-cream}} of cream and simmer for 3 minutes more. Remove from the heat. Stir in {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard. Taste, adjust seasoning, return the chicken to the sauce, and scatter with tarragon.
